title: Acres to Hectares Converter
tagline: Convert acres to hectares — and hectares back to acres — using the exact international conversion factor of 0.40468564224 hectares per acre.
intro: Enter a land area, pick the direction of conversion, and get an instant, surveyor-grade result you can use for farms, real estate listings, and international land deals.

type.acresToHectares_desc: Convert an area measured in acres (imperial / US customary) into hectares (SI metric). Useful when sharing a US property listing with European buyers, comparing US farm sizes to EU yield data, or filing international documents.
type.hectaresToAcres_desc: Convert an area measured in hectares into acres. Useful when reading metric-system survey data, EU CAP filings, or UN agricultural statistics and you need the equivalent acre value for a US audience.

error.invalid: Please enter a valid, non-negative number.

about.heading: About the Acres to Hectares Converter
about.body:
The acres to hectares converter is a precision land-area calculator that translates between the imperial acre and the metric hectare using the internationally accepted factor of 1 acre = 0.40468564224 hectares. The reverse direction uses 1 hectare = 2.4710538147 acres. Both values are derived from the exact relationship 1 international acre = 4,046.8564224 square metres, so the results match what land surveyors, civil engineers, and government cadastres use around the world.

Acres and hectares describe the same physical quantity — area — but they belong to two very different measurement traditions. The acre comes from medieval English agriculture, where it represented the amount of land a yoke of oxen could plough in a day. It survives today in the United States, the United Kingdom, India (where it sits alongside local units like bigha and gunta), Pakistan, Bangladesh, and several Commonwealth countries. One acre equals 43,560 square feet, 4,840 square yards, or 1⁄640 of a square mile. The hectare, by contrast, was created during the metric reforms of the French Revolution and is defined cleanly as 10,000 square metres — a 100 m × 100 m square. It is the official land-area unit of every European Union country, China, Russia, Brazil, Canada (alongside acres in real estate), Australia, and almost every African and Asian agricultural ministry.

Because the two units sit at the boundary between the imperial and metric worlds, conversions between them are needed constantly. A US farmer pricing a soybean field for an export contract has to quote yields in tonnes per hectare even though the field is registered in acres. A European real-estate fund buying a Texan ranch needs to convert the listed acreage into hectares to compare it against vineyards in Bordeaux or olive groves in Andalusia. Agricultural economists comparing the productivity of US corn against Brazilian soya, or UK wheat against Ukrainian wheat, must normalise everything into one unit before the numbers mean anything. This converter handles all of that with a single multiplication so you can move between the two systems without making rounding mistakes.

For most practical purposes, two to four decimal places of precision is plenty. A residential lot of 0.25 acres becomes 0.1012 hectares — close enough for a planning application. A 100-acre family farm becomes 40.4686 hectares, which lines up neatly with the European agricultural concept of a medium-sized holding. A 5,000-acre ranch becomes 2,023.43 hectares, immediately comparable to large estates in Argentina or Australia. When you need higher precision — for example, when registering land at a national cadastre or doing GIS work — the converter retains the full ten-significant-digit value internally and only rounds the displayed output.

Understanding the result is just as important as calculating it. A hectare is roughly the size of an international rugby pitch, or about 2.47 acres. An acre is about 4,047 square metres, or roughly the size of an American football field without the end zones. If you can picture those two reference shapes, you can sanity-check almost any conversion in your head. The exact factor used here (0.40468564224) is the one published by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the UK National Physical Laboratory, ensuring your results are legally consistent with surveyor reports, government records, and international trade documents.

howto.heading: How to convert acres to hectares
howto.step1: Identify your starting unit. Look at the source document — US deeds, MLS listings, and USDA reports use acres; EU land registries, FAO statistics, and most scientific papers use hectares.
howto.step2: Choose the conversion direction in the calculator: Acres → Hectares or Hectares → Acres.
howto.step3: Enter the numeric value with as much precision as your source provides. For surveyor reports keep four or more decimal places; for casual estimates two decimals is plenty.
howto.step4: Press Convert. The calculator multiplies by 0.40468564224 (acres to hectares) or 2.4710538147 (hectares to acres) and displays the rounded result along with the formula used.
howto.step5: Sanity-check the result: 1 hectare ≈ 2.47 acres, so the hectare figure should always be smaller than the acre figure for the same piece of land.

faq.heading: Frequently asked questions
faq.q1: What is the exact conversion factor from acres to hectares?
faq.q1_a: 1 international acre equals exactly 0.40468564224 hectares, because 1 acre is defined as 4,046.8564224 square metres and 1 hectare is exactly 10,000 square metres. The reverse factor, 1 hectare = 2.4710538147 acres, is simply the reciprocal.
faq.q2: Is the US acre the same as the UK acre?
faq.q2_a: For everyday work, yes. Both countries now use the international acre of 4,046.8564224 m². The historical US survey acre is about 4,046.8726 m², a difference of roughly 16 parts per million — negligible for almost every purpose except high-precision US federal land surveys.
faq.q3: How big is a hectare compared with an acre?
faq.q3_a: A hectare is about 2.47 times larger than an acre. Put another way, an acre is about 40% of a hectare. A hectare is a square 100 m on a side, while an acre is roughly the size of an American football field without the end zones.
faq.q4: How do I convert acres to square metres or square feet instead?
faq.q4_a: Multiply acres by 4,046.8564224 to get square metres, or by 43,560 to get square feet. Multiply hectares by 10,000 to get square metres, or by 107,639.104 to get square feet. This converter focuses on the acre ↔ hectare conversion, which is the most common need for international land deals.
faq.q5: How many decimal places should I keep in the result?
faq.q5_a: For real-estate listings and farm planning, two to four decimals is more than enough. For cadastral filings, GIS work, or legal documents, keep at least six decimals or use the raw value. The calculator stores ten significant digits internally so you don't lose precision when chaining further calculations.
faq.q6: Why do different sources sometimes give slightly different conversion factors?
faq.q6_a: Most discrepancies come from rounding. Some sources quote 0.4047 or 0.40469, which is fine for casual use but loses precision on large parcels. The acres to hectares converter uses the exact factor 0.40468564224 published by NIST, so a 10,000-acre estate converts to 4,046.8564224 hectares with no accumulated rounding error.
